content.problemStatement:
You are given a N√óMN \times MN√óM matrix AAA, filled with **distinct** numbers. You can start at any cell. If you are currently at cell (x1,y1)(x_1, y_1)(x1‚Äč,y1‚Äč), then you can jump to any other cell (x2,y2)(x_2, y_2)(x2‚Äč,y2‚Äč), if and only if the following two conditions are satisfied:



Ax2,y2>Ax1,y1A_{x_2, y_2} \gt A_{x_1, y_1}Ax2‚Äč,y2‚Äč‚Äč>Ax1‚Äč,y1‚Äč‚Äč




Manhattan distance between Ax2,y2A_{x_2, y_2}Ax2‚Äč,y2‚Äč‚Äč and Ax1,y1A_{x_1, y_1}Ax1‚Äč,y1‚Äč‚Äč >S \gt S>S. Formally, ‚ą£x1‚ąíx2‚ą£+‚ą£y1‚ąíy2‚ą£>S|x_1 - x_2| + |y_1 - y_2| \gt S‚ą£x1‚Äč‚ąíx2‚Äč‚ą£+‚ą£y1‚Äč‚ąíy2‚Äč‚ą£>S.



Let VVV be the set of the cells visited by you.

Maximize the value of ‚ąĎ(x,y)‚ąąV Ax,y\sum_{(x, y) \in V} \text{ }A_{x, y}‚ąĎ(x,y)‚ąąV‚Äč Ax,y‚Äč.


Input Format

The first line of the input contains the number TTT - the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The first line of each test case contains three integers N,M,SN, M, SN,M,S - the parameters defined in the statement.
Then NNN lines follow, with MMM integers in each line.
Each of the next NNN lines contains MMM integers, where the jjj-th integer in iii-th line is equal to Ai,jA_{i, j}Ai,j‚Äč


Output Format
For each testcase output the maximum possible value of ‚ąĎ(x,y)‚ąąV Ax,y\sum_{(x, y) \in V} \text{ }A_{x, y}‚ąĎ(x,y)‚ąąV‚Äč Ax,y‚Äč.
constraints: It's guaranteed that all Ai,jA_{i, j}Ai,j‚Äč are distinct.
It's guaranteed that sum of N√óMN \times MN√óM over all test cases doesn't exceed 10610^6106.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case-1: It is optimal start at cell (1,1)(1, 1)(1,1), then jump to cell (3,2)(3, 2)(3,2). Therefore answer is A1,1+A3,2=1+8=9A_{1, 1} + A_{3, 2} = 1 + 8 = 9A1,1‚Äč+A3,2‚Äč=1+8=9.
Test case-2: It is optimal to start at (2,2)(2, 2)(2,2) and follow this path: (2,2)‚Üí(2,1)‚Üí(1,2)‚Üí(1,1)(2, 2) \rightarrow (2, 1) \rightarrow (1, 2) \rightarrow (1, 1)(2,2)‚Üí(2,1)‚Üí(1,2)‚Üí(1,1). Therefore answer is the sum of all the four cells =394= 394=394.
